data.title: REITs Rally As Rates Retreat
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets slumped this past week, while Treasury yields pulled back sharply, as a confluence of cooling macro data and mixed corporate earnings results reinforced the value rotation and revived expectations of easing monetary policy. Yields tumbled after the critical Consumer Price Index report showed a continued cooldown in inflation to four-year lows, quelling concerns that tariff increases would lead to sustained upward price pressures. Labor market data landed squarely in “Goldilocks” territory – firm enough to quiet recession fears but soft enough to avoid reigniting rate anxiety – while softer retail sales underscored a gradual cooling in consumer momentum. Together, these forces accelerated the value rotation push, benefiting cyclicals and income-oriented sectors at the expense of AI-exposed tech sectors.

Posting declines for the fourth time in the past five weeks, the S&P 500 slipped 1.2% on the week, erasing its year-to-date gains. Smaller-cap and rate-sensitive segments outperformed for a second straight week, continuing the "value rotation" dynamic that has been the key equity market theme of early 2026. The Small-Cap 600 and Mid-Cap 400 posted more modest declines this week, maintaining their year-to-date gains at about 8%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 slumped 1% - now down about 5% from its recent-highs – amid ongoing concern about the AI impact on the software industry itself. Buoyed by the rate retreat, real estate equities posted sizable outperformance this week as earnings season kicked into high gear with strong reports from data center, senior housing, and retail REITs, offsetting some weakness from office and multifamily REITs. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.5% on the week, with 14 of 20 property sectors in positive territory. The Housing Index advanced 1% as strength from homebuilders on rate optimism and M&A news helped offset sharp pressure on brokerage firms.

In bond markets, Treasury yields retreated across the curve for a second straight week as traders responded to the deluge of macro data by tempering expectations of an imminent Fed rate cut but pricing in a higher quantity of cuts later this year. Closing at the lowest levels since August 2022, the 2-Year Treasury Yield dipped 9 basis points to close at 3.41%. The 10-Year Treasury Yield plunged 16 basis points to 4.05%, down sharply from the intraday highs above 4.30% in late January and back within range of its three-year lows of 3.95% seen last October. While traders remain skeptical of a March rate cut – with odds dipping to 10% this week from 185 last week – swaps markets now price in a median Fed Funds Rate of 3.11% at the end of 2026, implying 2.5 rate cuts for the year, a considerably more dovish outlook compared to the 2.2 rate cuts implied last week. In commodities and currency markets, energy prices continued to moderate as temperatures warmed amid the coldest winter in decades for many East Coast cities. Crude Oil prices declined 1% to around $61/barrel, while Natural Gas spot prices tumbled for a third straight week. Gold prices advanced nearly 2% for a second straight week, while Bitcoin rebounded from two-year lows, both stabilizing after a late-January selloff across the trendy "dollar debasement" plays.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

More good news on the inflation front. The critical Consumer Price Index this week surprised to the downside once again, despite some snap-back effects from the shutdown-impacted reports in prior months and some historical upward bias in recent early-year inflation reports. Headline CPI increased 2.39% from a year ago in January – the second-coolest annual increase of the past four years – and below consensus estimates of 2.50%. Core CPI – which excludes food and energy – increased 2.50% from a year ago, down from 2.64% in December, which marked the lowest level of core inflation since March 2021. For context, the Fed Funds rate in March 2021 was at the zero lower bound and remained there for the subsequent year, until Core CPI surged to 6.5% in March 2022. Perhaps most importantly, the CPI-ex-Shelter Index – the metric we follow most closely, given the substantial distortions from the heavily weighted (and arguably miscalculated) shelter component – cooled to 2.04% in January, the lowest since June.

We've highlighted for several years how the CPI's heavy weighting in the shelter category – combined with a 12-18 month sampling lag for these housing categories – resulted in a substantial understating of inflation as rents and home prices surged in 2021 and 2022, and a subsequent overstating of inflation since 2023 as real-time housing inflation has cooled substantially to near zero. For that reason, we compile a Real Time CPI using the Zillow Home Price Index (ZVHI) and Zillow Rent Index (ZORI) as a substitute for the BLS' indexes for primary rent and owner's equivalent rent. This adjusted CPI shows that inflation has averaged 2.0% since late 2023. While the Shelter component in CPI has moderated in recent months, it remained the most significant upside contributor in January, responsible for 39% of the monthly increase and 45% of the annual increase in the CPI index. Besides shelter, airfares jumped +6.5% m/m, contributing roughly 30% of the monthly CPI increase, while hospital services (+0.9%) also added modest upward pressure. Offsetting these gains, energy prices were a meaningful drag, led by a -3.2% m/m decline in gasoline, which alone subtracted roughly half of the month’s CPI increase. Used cars and trucks (01.8%) continued to deflate, while motor vehicle insurance and fuel oil also provided downside offsets.

Following a wave of surprisingly cool labor market data last week, the BLS Nonfarm Payrolls report - which was delayed a week by a second (brief) government shutdown in early February - surprised to the upside in January, pushing back on concerns of a significant deterioration in labor market conditions. Employers added 130k jobs in January, the strongest month of hiring since December 2024 and well above consensus expectations of +66k. The prior two months, however, were revised lower by 17k combined, while the annual benchmark revisions made sizable downward revisions to employment figures in 2024 and 2025, totaling -862k - slightly worse than the consensus of a -850k downward revision. For January, private payroll gains were notably strong at +172k, offset by a -42k dip in government employment. The Unemployment Rate edged down to 4.3%, while the Participation Rate improved slightly. Average Hourly Earnings rose 0.4% in January, bringing full-year wage growth to 3.7%, still outpacing inflation but consistent with the gradual cooling trend. Sector-level details continued to show a bifurcated labor market, with strength concentrated in defensive and service-oriented industries (educational and healthcare, in particular), while cyclical sectors have softened in recent months.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Real estate earnings season kicked into high gear this week, with reports from roughly three dozen REITs, which pushed us to the halfway point of earnings season. As discussed in our REIT Earnings Halftime Report (available to our Investing Group subscribers), aided by favorable "goldilocks" macro data trends – cooling inflation alongside "good enough" labor market data – REITs have delivered notable earnings season outperformance thus far over the S&P 500. Underlying REIT results have been solid, with no major bombshells and several upside surprises. 51% of REITs reported 2025 FFO beating their full-year outlook, with better trends in underlying property-level metrics. Data Center, Senior Housing, and Retail REITs have been the upside standouts. AI-fueled demand has kept data center fundamentals tight despite record new supply. Senior Housing has remained red-hot. Retail REIT results have also been a pleasant upside surprise. Limited new supply has kept occupancy rates near record highs, and rent spreads in double digits despite a slight demand moderation. Apartment, Office, and Commercial Mortgage REITs have been the notable laggards. Multifamily rents softened in late 2025, particularly in the Sunbelt. Office REIT results showed progress at the property level but lingering headwinds on bottom-line FFO from balance sheet repair. Results from Commercial mREIT have been a setback after several encouraging quarters, as pockets of distress lingered in office, multifamily, and lab space loans, stretching dividend coverage. Below, we discuss the notable highlights and lowlights of the past week.

Data Center: Beginning with the upside standouts this week, Equinix (EQIX)—the largest data center REIT – surged 13% after it delivered a decisive earnings beat, boosted its dividend by 10%, and introduced a 2026 outlook that meaningfully exceeded expectations. Fueled by robust enterprise, hyperscale, and AI-driven demand, EQIX reported normalized AFFO growth of 9.5% in 2025 – matching its prior guidance – and forecasts 10.4% AFFO growth in 2026, well above consensus expectations and a sharp step-up from the 5% growth profile outlined at its June Analyst Day. Bookings surged 42% year-over-year to $474M, supported by strong enterprise and AI-related demand, with an additional $170M of pre-sales exiting 2025 and another ~$100M signed early in 2026. Consistent with the report last week from its peer Digital Realty (DLR), pricing power remained very strong despite booming supply. EQIX reported same-store recurring revenue growth of 6% - the strongest since late 2023 - while MRR per cabinet increased 8%. While interconnection net adds of 4,700 came in modestly below recent averages, EQIX expects to deliver approximately 18,000 cabinets in 2026 and nearly 26,000 in 2027. Iron Mountain (IRM) surged 15% after reporting a stellar quarter for its growing data center segment -which now accounts for roughly a third of its revenues - and surprisingly strong double-digit revenue growth in its legacy business storage segment. Fueled by 30% revenue growth in its data center segment, IRM delivered full-year FFO growth of nearly 14% in 2025 and guided to growth of another 11% in 2026, expecting at least 25% revenue growth in its data center segment based on its current backlog.

Senior Housing: Welltower (WELL) - the largest senior housing owner in the country and top-performing REIT on a rolling five-year basis – surged 8% this week after reporting another stellar quarter and providing a very strong 2026 outlook, fueled by continued outperformance in its Senior Housing Operating Portfolio (“SHOP”). WELL reported full-year FFO growth of 22.5% in 2025 – above its prior guidance of 18.1% – and forecasts these robust trends to continue in 2026 with an outlook calling for FFO growth of 16.6% this year. Senior Housing NOI grew 21.5% in 2025 – topping its prior guidance – and WELL forecasts growth of 18.0% in 2026 (VTR: 15%, DOC: 13%), driven by further occupancy gains (+350 bps expected) and sustained pricing momentum (5% RevPOR growth). WELL continues to be among the most active REITs on the external-growth front, highlighting $5.7B of investments closed or under contract year-to-date, the launch of a $2.5B senior housing private fund, and the $1.3B sale of a portion of its Integra portfolio, further simplifying the portfolio toward a pure-play SHOP strategy. Sabra Healthcare (SBRA) – which owns a mix of senior housing and skilled nursing assets – rallied 6% this week after reporting in-line fourth-quarter results but provided a forecast indicating 5%+ FFO growth in 2026. While its SHOP segment remains the outperformer, the skilled nursing business continued to strengthen at the margins in late 2025 via occupancy and rent coverage improvement.

Medical Office: Sticking in the healthcare space, Healthcare Realty (HR) – the second-largest Medical Office Building ("MOB") REIT – surged 6% this week after it reported better-than-expected results, driven by a late-year acceleration in leasing activity. HR delivered full-year FFO growth of 3.5% for 2025 – topping its prior forecast of 2.3% – which snapped a stretch of three straight annual FFO declines. Strong leasing volume in Q4 drove a +100 bps year-over-year increase in occupancy, with base rents up 4.7%. Citing a favorable supply-demand balance from a sharp pullback in new development, HR delivered its strongest pace of full-year NOI growth since before the pandemic. Its core MOB segment delivered 4.8% NOI growth in 2025 – above its prior forecast of 4.4% – which was consistent with the fourth-quarter MOB outperformance reported earlier in earnings season from Welltower and Healthpeak. Guidance calls for flat FFO growth in 2026 at the midpoint of its initial range, as solid NOI growth of 4% is offset by some headwinds from the $1B+ in asset sales over the past year, which helped it reset its leverage to a very healthy 5.4x Debt/EBITDA multiple from 6.4x at the start of the year.

Net Lease: Boosted by the pullback in interest rates, the net lease sector delivered a very strong week as results from seven REITs showed an acceleration in acquisition activity – and in bottom-line FFO growth – as investment spreads become more accretive. Getty Realty (GTY) – which focuses on gas stations and other automotive-related real estate – jumped 8% after reporting solid fourth quarter results highlighted by a notable acceleration in acquisition activity. GTY completed $269M of investments in 2025 (up 28% from 2024), including $135M in Q4 at a 7.9% cap rate. WP Carey (WPC) – the second-largest net lease REIT – gained 4% after reporting solid results that capped off an active 2025 as it completed a multi-year strategic portfolio repositioning to focus more exclusively on industrial real estate. Fueled by embedded contractual escalators and robust investment activity, WPC reported full-year FFO growth of 5.7% in 2025 and forecast FFO growth of 4.2% for full-year 2026. Essentials Property (EPRT) – which has been the top-performing net lease REIT on a rolling five-year basis – gained 1% after reporting sector-leading FFO growth in 2025 and raised its already upbeat outlook for 2026. Agree Realty (ADC) and National Retail (NNN) – the third- and fourth-largest net lease REITs – each gained about 3% after reporting similarly solid results that topped prior guidance. On the downside, Four Corners (FCPT) – which focuses on restaurants – dipped 3% after reporting modestly disappointing results amid a slight uptick in missed rents.

Strip Center: Results from a half-dozen strip center REITs this week showed a continuation of robust retail real estate fundamentals in late 2025 and into 2026, with the sector on pace for a 15th-straight quarter of double-digit leasing spreads. Kimco (KIM) – the largest strip center REIT – rallied 4% this week after reporting solid fourth-quarter results, highlighted by a record-high occupancy rate driven by strength across both its anchor and small-shop segments. Leasing trends remained healthy, highlighted by 2.7M SF of leasing activity in Q4 2025 and blended cash leasing spreads of +13.8% (TTM: 13.0%) – its 10th straight quarter of double-digit spreads. Brixmor (BRX) gained 3% – which owns a regionally diverse portfolio focused on grocery-anchored centers – gained 3% after reporting similarly solid results, highlighted by an impressive +24% blended spread (an acceleration from its TTM rate of +20%), led by strong in-line leasing that pushed its overall leased rate to 95.1%, just below record highs for the company. InvenTrust (IVT) – which focuses on grocery-anchored centers in the Southeast – gained 1% this week after reporting solid results and hiking its dividend by 5%. On the downside, Acadia Realty (AKR) – which owns a high concentration of street-level retail in urban markets – dipped 2% this week as investors focus on a less-than-clean earnings mix distorted by some special items related primarily to its Albertsons investment. Urban Edge (UE) – which focuses on the Northeast region with a skew towards large power centers – slipped 1% this week despite hiking its dividend by 11% alongside an in-line earnings report. Federal Realty (FRT) - which owns a more "mixed-use" portfolio with 10% of its portfolio in residential and office - also slipped 1% this week as strong retail leasing was partially offset by softer trends in its other segments.

Apartment: Results from Apartment REITs have been modestly disappointing, with results showing the early-year reacceleration in multifamily rent growth completely reversed in the back half of the year, even in the previously hot Northeast and Midwest markets. UDR Inc. (UDR) – the fifth-largest apartment REIT with the most regionally diverse portfolios among its peers – gained 1% this week after reporting mixed Q4 results but provided a relatively constructive outlook for 2026. UDR reported blended rent growth of -2.1% in Q4 (-7.2% on new leases and +2.7% on renewals) – the weakest among the five apartment REITs – but UDR emphasized that it was aggressively discounting rents toward a high occupancy strategy, which did indeed lift its same-store occupancy rate to 96.9% – up 20 basis points from a year ago. At the regional level, the West was the clear relative outperformer, delivering +0.9% blended growth. The Northeast and Mid-Atlantic saw modest blended declines (-1.5% and -2.5%), while the Southeast and Southwest experienced the sharpest softness, driven by double-digit new-lease declines and flat renewal growth. Encouragingly, turnover declined meaningfully across all regions year-over-year, consistent with trends observed across the sector. UDR noted that blended lease growth improved steadily through Q4 and turned positive in January (+1.0%) and is guided to +1.5–2.0% in 1Q26. Sunbelt-focused Independence (IRT) slipped 1.5% after reporting mixed results, as a surprising reacceleration in blended spreads in late 2025 - the only REIT to report a sequential uptick – was offset by a muted FFO outlook for 2026 on higher interest expense and lower development yields.

Office: On the downside this week, results from a half-dozen office REITs showed that while property-level trends have inflected positively—evidenced by improved leasing volumes, modest occupancy gains, and stabilizing in-place rents—bottom-line profitability remains constrained by elevated leverage, higher interest expense, and balance sheets still healing from years of value erosion. West Coast-focused Kilroy (KRC) dipped 9% after reporting a transitional 4Q25, as decent leasing volumes were overshadowed by a meaningful reset in 2026 earnings expectations. KRC reported that its full-year FFO declined -8.5% in 2025 – below its prior guide - and its 2026 outlook calls for a steep -20.2% FFO decline in 2026. Leasing volumes were the bright spot, with 827K SF signed during the quarter (well above its TTM average of around 475k SF), but cash leasing spreads deteriorated to -27.1% in the quarter, its worst on record. Douglas Emmett (DEI) – which focuses exclusively on the L.A. and Honolulu office markets – slipped 5% after reporting similar trends. DEI reported a full-year FFO dip of -15.2% in 2025 and forecasts another down year in 2026, with FFO expected to decline by -2.1% this year. DEI signed 906k SF in Q4 2025, up nearly 14% year-over-year and modestly above its TTM average, but cash leasing spreads dipped -10.1% – its fourth straight quarter with double-digit spread declines. Sunbelt-focused Highwoods (HIW) dipped 10% after decent Q4 results and a constructive 2026 outlook were offset by a subsequent $300M equity issuance. HIW reported a full-year FFO decline of -3.6% in 2025 but forecasts a return to growth in 2026, with expectations of 1.7% FFO growth this year. Vornado (VNO) – the second largest office REIT – slipped 4% after it reported a mixed Q4 2025, with improving fundamentals in its core New York office portfolio offset by some property-specific headwinds.

Industrial: Also among the laggards this week, LXP Industrial (LXP) dipped 7% this week after reporting mixed fourth-quarter results, as a late-year rebound in leasing activity wasn't enough to offset a very slow start to the year amid tariff uncertainty. LXP reported an FFO decline of -1.6% in 2025 – below its prior forecast of -0.8% – its seventh annual FFO decline in the past nine years. None of the six largest industrial REITs has reported a single annual FFO decline during this period. Despite a solid leasing pace in Q4, LXP ultimately leased 5M SF of space in 2025 – down 26% from last year - and has 41% of its 2026 rent rollover renewed compared to the roughly 55% at this time last year. LXP commented that "it continues to be a real challenge to get deals done, lots of RFP traffic... really just getting them across the finish line." As a result, same-store occupancy dipped to 97.3% at year-end, down 220 basis points from last year. LXP – which focuses primarily on single-tenant triple net leased properties - reported disappointing 8.8% leasing spreads, weighed down by several fixed-rate renewals. (22.6% excluding these fixed renewals). STAG Industrial (STAG) declined 3% despite reporting a solid quarter of leasing activity and a corresponding boost to its same-store occupancy. STAG reported full-year FFO growth of 6.7% in 2025 - well above its prior guidance of 5.4%. STAG has addressed 69% of its 2026 expirations – roughly in line with its pace at this time last year – and expects cash leasing spreads of 20% in 2026, only a modest step down from the 25% spreads achieved in 2025. STAG provided a relatively muted outlook for 2026, however, with expectations of 2.2% FFO growth this year, reflecting an occupancy headwind of 120 basis points (vs. -100 bps previously).

Commercial mREITs: While still early in earnings season for the sector, results across the commercial mortgage space have been disappointing thus far, as pockets of loan distress have lingered in the office, multifamily, and life science sectors, pressuring book values and stretching dividend coverage. Franklin BSP Realty (FBRT) – which focuses primarily on the typically steady multifamily space – dipped 13% this week after it replaced its CEO and announced a 43% reduction in its quarterly dividend. While its book value was relatively steady in Q4 – down 1% from Q3 – FBRT reported that its quantity of nonperforming loans in its core portfolio jumped from 3 to 7 in the quarter, including the foreclosure on a 272-unit apartment complex in Fort Worth, TX. FBRT noted that the dividend "reset" was driven by several factors:

...the recent declines in SOFR, the timing of our originations and repayments, and the overall size of our loan portfolio. In addition, spreads are at multi-decade tights, and REO liquidations are taking longer than originally anticipated.

The soft results dragged on its yet-to-report multifamily-focused peers as well, including Ready Capital (RC), which dipped more than 10% on the week. KKR Real Estate (KREF) was also under pressure this week after reporting similarly soft results with a pair of downgrades on a Cambridge life science property and a San Diego multifamily property and noted that its dividend policy was under review. Results from Blackstone Mortgage (BXMT) this week were notably stronger, however, with no significant incremental loan distress issues reported by either lender. BXMT highlighted 99% performance in its loan portfolio, up from from 96% in Q3.

2026 YTD Performance & 2025 Recap

Through the six weeks of 2026, REITs have outperformed the large-cap benchmarks by a sizable margin, driven by a broader value rotation that has buoyed the small- and mid-cap segment. The Equity REIT Index has posted gains of 6.9% this year, the Mortgage REIT Index has gained 2.9%, and the Housing 100 Index has rallied 9.1%. This compares to the fractional declines for the S&P 500, the more impressive 7.9% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 8.9% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 16 of 20 property sectors are higher on the year, led by Farmland, Data Center, and Net Lease REITs. At 4.05%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now lower by 11 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield is lower by 7 basis points at 3.41%. Rates at the longest end of the yield curve have pulled back more significantly, with the 30-Year Treasury Yield lower by 15 basis points to 4.70%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is higher by 1.3% this year after notching gains of 7% in 2025. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil has rebounded by 8% this year to around $61/barrel after dipping 12% in 2025. Gold has rallied nearly 16% after soaring by over 60% last year, while Bitcoin has dipped 20% after declining 6% in 2025.

Looking back on 2025, real estate equities significantly lagged the major equity benchmarks, notching a fourth straight year of underperformance versus all the major equity benchmarks - the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 - for the first time ever. For REITs - the most interest rate-sensitive market segment – the underperformance aligns with notable weakness in ultra-long duration assets amid lingering inflation uncertainty. The REIT sector finished in the basement among the ten major asset classes - its second time in the bottom spot in the past four years and the third time in the past six years. During the preceding decade from 2010–2019, the REIT sector had never finished worse than sixth among the ten major asset classes. As discussed in our State of REIT Nation (available to our Investing Group subscribers), REITs have endured a brutal three-plus-year stretch since March 2022, underperforming the S&P 500 by over 70 percentage points, a magnitude that's more than twice as significant as the underperformance gap seen during the 2007-2009 Global Financial Crisis.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

Following a hectic week of major economic reports, we'll see another busy slate of economic data in the holiday-shortened week ahead. The most closely watched reports come on Friday, beginning with the first look at fourth-quarter Gross Domestic Product. GDP data is expected to show that the U.S. economy grew at an annualized growth rate of 3% in the fourth quarter and for the full year, returning to a more normalized level following a Q1 and Q2–Q3 rebound on tariff distortions in import/export trends. We'll also see the delayed December PCE Price Index - the Fed's preferred inflation gauge – which is expected to show inflation rates hovering in the mid-to-high-2% range, down from the pandemic peak of 7%, but still above the Fed's stated 2.0% policy objective. Earlier in the week, on Tuesday, we'll see NAHB Homebuilder Confidence data, which is expected to show subdued sentiment in early 2026 despite the pullback in mortgage rates, with forecasts for a 22nd-straight month below the neutral 50-level. On Wednesday, we'll see the delayed Housing Starts report for both November and December, which are expected to show correspondingly sluggish construction activity in late 2025. The report is expected to confirm that total starts declined for a fourth straight year in 2025 following an early-pandemic boom in activity. On Thursday, we'll see Pending Home Sales for January, which are expected to show more upbeat recent trends in activity in the existing sales market as mortgage rates briefly dipped under 6% in late January before rebounding to the 6.10%-6.20% range in recent weeks. On Friday, we'll see the delayed New Home Sales report for both November and December. We'll also be watching the weekly employment metrics via ADP and Jobless Claims and a handful of regional and national PMI surveys throughout the week.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Centers, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Esperion Therapeutics: Past The Turnaround, Waiting On Guidelines
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Esperion Therapeutics, Inc. (ESPR) is a successful turnaround story, and it has nearly doubled on the basis. We must understand, though, that while the share price has recovered, its commercial turnaround is still an ongoing event. So the stock is right now in a waiting phase rather than a momentum phase. What it is waiting for - that key catalyst - is US guideline inclusion.

Esperion has nicely de-risked its business model. Commercial traction is happening, they have almost fully controlled the generic problems, and management is guiding towards sustainable profits by early 2026. However, real repricing will likely need positive guideline inclusion - like we saw in Europe recently. That alone would structurally change prescribing behavior and payer dynamics. Only then will the market fully reprice Esperion as a durable cardiovascular franchise.

So it makes little sense to trim ahead of this important catalyst, but adding now is also harder to justify given the rally we just went through, plus the small if critical risk that the guidelines will not be favorable.

What Has Changed Since The Bear Case Broke

Esperion has traded along a narrow fault line for most of its life - on the one side of that line were its approved CV drugs, and on the other hand, the persistent generic overhang. That situation has now largely been resolved through a number of ANDA settlements.

First, Esperion has settled with multiple ANDA filers, including Dr. Reddy’s (RDY). They now have a favorable agreement from settled filers whereby no generic entry is possible before April 2040. This transforms Nexletol and Nexlizet from mid-cycle assets to cash-flow-generating products with long shelf lives.

Second, Bempedoic acid’s label expansion has taken it out of a narrow adjunct population and broadened it across primary and secondary prevention. The opportunity is now no longer niche but mass-market. Cardiovascular drugs are sticky once there are supporting guidelines, so we can expect usage to persist.

Third, commercial performance used to be good in some quarters bad in others. That’s no longer the case. They are seeing sustained prescription growth, there’s much more improved payer access, and patients are paying less and less out of pocket. Esperion does not need to discount aggressively - growth is now coming from access and adoption, so revenue quality has improved.

Lastly, their balance sheet has recovered strongly. They still have leverage and royalty obligations, but their near-term liquidity risk is gone. ESPR’s balance sheet always used to be in existential crisis mode. Today, management is talking about making profits - that too, not as a one-time affair through milestone payments and so on, but from an operating perspective. This is real progress.

So, Esperion is no longer a turnaround story; however, there’s still some risk before the market can accept it as a mature cardiovascular company supported by guidelines. This is why I rate it a Hold.

The Real Catalyst: US Guideline Inclusion Is the Valuation Unlock

As I just noted, the updated US ACC/AHA cholesterol management guidelines will come out in Q1 2026. Management has been clear that this is a key catalyst for the company. Guidelines matter greatly in cardiovascular medicine because prescribers are often conservative.

The CLEAR Outcomes trial has made Esperion’s chances of favorable inclusion much better today. The trial demonstrated statistically significant reductions in cardiovascular events in statin-intolerant patients. This is a much better and harder outcome than surrogate LDL-only evidence. This data finally makes a non-statin therapy reach the gold standard of cardiovascular outcomes (CVOT) trials and is the reason the ESC/EAS guidelines turned favorable.

If the same thing works in the US, increases in TAM will be meaningful. Prior to the outcomes data and label expansion, Nexletol and Nexlizet were largely confined to a narrow adjunct population, estimated at roughly 10 million patients. Once primary and secondary prevention became included, the addressable population went up to ~70 million patients. All sorts of statin optionality come into play now - individuals who are statin-intolerant, insufficiently controlled on statins, or unwilling to escalate statin dosing all fall under the current label.

Yet, there’s still risk - US guidelines tend to be more conservative than European ones, so bempedoic acid could well get positioned as a later-line option, not a foundational therapy. Apart from that, there’s competition from PCSK9 inhibitors and inclisiran, which could also inform the later vs. earlier line decision for non-statin oral therapies. Even a little caution in wording could hamper the drug’s adoption and, by extension, the re-rating of ESPR.

This is the real reason the stock has lost some of the momentum it displayed in the early months of last year. The company has done well in terms of execution, but this is not out of its hands now. That final confirmation is what the market is waiting for to reprice ESPR as a durable cardiovascular franchise.

Why This Is Still Not A Buy

Despite ESPR’s improving fundamentals, let us consider the following reasons for making it a Hold and not a Buy.

Modest Revenue Growth: Revenue is growing, but at a modest pace. In Q3 2025, total revenue increased approximately 69% year-over-year to $87.3 million, with U.S. net product revenue up about 31% to roughly $40.7 million. For 1H 2025, net product sales of Nexletol and Nexlizet totaled $75.2 million, up from $53.1 million the year prior.

Operating Leverage: Gross margins and operating leverage also remain modest. ESPR is not yet consistently profitable on a net basis. Their outcomes keep varying from quarter to quarter, and while they are nearing profitability, they are not consistently there.

Royalty Obligations: Esperion sold a portion of its European bempedoic acid royalties for $304.7 million upfront to OMERS Life Sciences. OMERS to receive a 15-25% royalty on European net sales until 1.7x that capital (~$518 million) is paid back. After that, royalties revert back to Esperion. The deal did materially improve liquidity and removed secured liens - much needed at that time - but it does divert a decent share of European cash flows for the next few years. This is going to limit near-term free cash flow conversion.

Debt And Interest Costs: Besides the royalty burden, Esperion also has a $150 million term loan with a high floating interest rate (9.75% to 11.75%) and convertible notes totaling $100 million due in 2030. Interest expenses totaled nearly $40 million over the first six months of 2025. This burden is a hangover from when Esperion was a struggling early commercial biopharma and is still a drag on the company’s profitability even as revenue grows.

Prescription Growth: The company is seeing strong prescription growth, driving net product sales in a sustained manner. Esperion reported ~34% year-over-year growth in retail prescription figures, translating to ~31% YoY growth in US net product revenue. That comes to around $40.7 million in Q3 2025. More than 45,000 physicians have prescribed Nexletol or Nexlizet, and that is supported by ~90% commercial and Medicare coverage.

However, absolute quarterly product revenue is still low.

Taken together, and stacking them against the stock’s near 100% surge in the last 12 months, these are some of the reasons Esperion is not yet a Buy. While execution has been strong, its royalty/debt obligations, prescription growth, etc., make its valuation still a work in progress.

Valuation: What ESPR Is Worth Before And After Guidelines

A proper valuation framework for Esperion should consider it a single-franchise cardiovascular company that is just transitioning from recovery to durability. Revenue is surely growing, but the more important question is how much of that is retained, given the leverage and royalty structures.

Per their recent JPM conference, they noted that they exited 2025 with “a range of $156 million to $160 million. Total revenue of $400 million to $408 million. Our retail prescriptions equivalent grew 34% year-over-year.” Now, we have two scenarios: pre- and post-guideline. A reasonable pre-guideline base case assumes:

  • US product revenue grows to $220–250 million over the next 12–18 months.

  • They can expect incremental upside from supply revenue and international royalties, constrained by their OMERS royalty obligation.

Under these assumptions, we can estimate peak sales scenarios at $350 million (conservative), $500 million (base case), and $650+ million (bull case). The conservative scenario assumes only later-line positioning and slower adoption. The base case assumes less dominant guideline inclusion, while the bull case takes in early-line inclusion with broader payer coverage.

For a Hold rating, the base case represents the right scenario.

Now, Esperion says they will attain sustainable profitability in early 2026. However, I doubt operating margins will reach big pharma figures anytime soon. I would assume gross margins to be in the 70-75% range, operating margin at 25-30%, and EBITDA margin at around 30-35%. At $500 million peak US revenue, that implies $125-150 million operating income before interests, royalties, and taxes.

Those last couple of items are holding it back. Royalty sale liability is nearly ~$300 million, debt+converts together are ~$250 million, and ongoing interest expense is around ~$40-45 million annually in the near term. Adjusting for these, I get sustainable equity-free cash flow at around $60–80 million.

Now, considering a single-asset cardiovascular company with no generic challenge until 2040, moderate growth, and a leveraged balance sheet, a fair multiple is 10-12x FCF or 8-10x EBITDA. When I apply that to $70 million normalized FCF, I get equity value in the range of $700–850 million.

Taking ~235 million fully diluted shares, that gives me a per-share, pre-guideline base case of $3.00-3.50 per share. For this to get to $5-7 per share, we need to have clear guideline inclusion, and then faster adoption, and lastly, clear margin expansion.

The above discussion explains why ESPR is trading where it is now - roughly fair value after significant upward movement. This price does not reflect what might happen post-guideline.

What To Expect From Q4 Earnings

Q4 results are on March 3, and while they won’t change the core investment debate, I will be looking for indications of durability. I expect year-over-year topline growth to continue in US net product revenue. I also expect Rx to grow, along with payer access. These are YoY expectations, although I am less sanguine about sequential growth, which might remain uneven due to various costs in legal, commercial, and so on.

More important for me is any color on margin progression and expense control. I will look forward to management commentary on these, including gross margin improvement and SG&A trends. To me, improvements here are more important than whether they make a narrow profit or loss in Q4, which I view more as a setup quarter, which will reinforce whether Esperion will become a sustainably profitable concern in 2026.

Lastly, of course, is the guidelines inclusion. If that happens before earnings, that will significantly change my interest in the management commentary.

Risks

There are several risks. The most critical is any negative outcome of the US guideline aspect. Outcomes data is good, Europe sets a good precedent, but the US is often more conservative. Even minor differences in wording could slow things down for Esperion.

The second risk is the financial obligations from its earlier phase and how that drags on future free cash flow. These encumbrances are not letting the company come out of its sheath at its current level of revenue generation. They must be able to create operating profitability after servicing these claims.

They also have significant execution risks. They need to be able to continue with their sales outreach so prescription growth is sustained and payer adoption increases. Lastly, we cannot entirely ignore competitive risks. PCSK9 inhibitors and inclisiran are major competitors. While bempedoic acid is well-differentiated as an oral option, changes in pricing or access or guidelines can hamper its growth.

Bottom Line

Esperion has been a turnaround story in 2025, removing many of the risks from its business. However, a true repricing is linked to the US dyslipidemia guidelines, and despite the strong CVOT data, the results are unpredictable. Given that the stock has significantly gone up in the last 12 months, it is more prudent to Hold the stock right now than to outright Buy. Admittedly, if the guidelines turn positive, a Buy now will yield strong profits, but given my personal risk appetite, I will wait this one out.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp: Dividend Value Stock In Buy Range
data.description:
Recent volatility in software stocks like Adobe (ADBE) and Workday (WDAY) that don’t pay a dividend can make it hard to hold onto these names, especially for investors who seek to regularly cash out of stocks as a source of income. That’s why I’m generally sticking to dividend growth stocks that remain reasonably priced for sleep-well-at-night returns.

Such I find the case with U.S. Bancorp (USB), which I last covered a while back in October 2024, highlighting its strong risk management and steady loan and deposit growth. At the time, USB carried a forward P/E of 12.0 and has delivered a 32% total return, far outpacing the 17% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same time frame.

At the current price of $57.69 and 11.5 forward P/E, USB actually has a lower forward P/E than when I covered it last, thanks to rising earnings. It also pays a respectable 3.6% dividend yield, which is more than 3x that of the S&P 500.

In this article, I revisit USB, including recent business results, and discuss why it remains a solid value ‘Buy’ at present for potentially strong total returns in this volatile market, so let’s dig in!

Why USB?

U.S. Bancorp is a leading diversified financial services holding company that’s sometimes referred to as America’s largest regional bank. It offers a broad suite of banking and financial services, including consumer and business banking, payment services, and wealth management. It has one of the country’s largest branch networks and comprehensive digital platforms.

While the market whipsaws back and forth, USB continues to churn out reliable results. This is reflected by net revenue growth of 5.1% YoY during Q4 2025 to reach a record $7.4 billion. This was driven by net interest income growth of 3.3% YoY as consumer deposits continue to grow. Return on tangible common equity was a strong 18.4%, and net interest margin improved by 6 basis points YoY to 2.77%.

Fee revenue grew by 7.6% YoY due to broad-based strength across payments and institutional services. Notably, fee income now represents 42% of USB’s net revenue. This is a net positive, as this reduces USB’s reliance on pure interest rate spread (the difference between interest on loans versus interest paid to depositors). As such, USB’s revenue stream is becoming more stable and less sensitive to interest rates.

At the same time, USB is demonstrating disciplined expense management, with the efficiency ratio improving from the low-60% range to ~57% over the past 2 quarters, as shown below. The efficiency ratio is calculated by taking adjusted operating expenses divided by revenue, so the lower the better.

Management is guiding for 4% to 6% revenue growth in 2026, excluding incremental revenues from the BTIG acquisition. Operating leverage is also expected to grow by at least 200 basis points, driven by fee-based revenue growth. Expense management remains a priority for USB as it expects to continue with productivity initiatives as it has had over the past 9 quarters.

Importantly, the acquisition of BTIG, a global financial services firm, is expected to help drive USB’s fee-based revenue growth along with operating leverage. USB is already familiar with BTIG’s operations, as it’s had a 10-year partnership that includes the completion of 350 deals. Notably, global financial services is a highly capital-efficient business within USB, and the combination with BTIG adds to that model, as noted during the recent earnings call:

The products offered by GFS attract high-quality operational deposits, money market assets under management and capital markets business such as foreign exchange. BTIG capabilities will further support growth in this business. GFS total net revenue has grown at a healthy 11% CAGR since 2021 and grew at 12% in 2025.

USB maintains a solid balance sheet with an ‘A’ credit rating from S&P. It has a common equity tier 1 capital ratio of 10.8%, which sits well in excess of the 4.5% requirement. The stock currently yields 3.6%, and the dividend is well-covered by a 44% payout ratio. It also comes with 15 consecutive years of growth and a 5-year CAGR of 4%.

I continue to see value in USB at the current price of $57.69 with a forward P/E of 11.5, sitting below its historical P/E of 13.4, as shown below.

At the current valuation, the market does not appear to fully appreciate USB’s steady growth and potential for margin expansion through growth in its GFS segment. This is further supported by its upcoming BTIG acquisition, which enhances the scale of the GFS segment. Analysts estimate 8% to 10% annual EPS growth over the next 2 years. Combined with the 3.6% dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, USB could deliver total returns in the teens over the medium term.

Risks to USB include the potential for credit losses in the commercial real estate sector in the event of a macroeconomic downturn. Lower property valuations could result in default risk should borrowers be unable to make payments. Lower interest rates on loans also pose a risk should USB be unable to offset that with lower rates on deposit balances. Higher capital requirements by regulators could also result in lower returns to shareholders.

Investor Takeaway

U.S. Bancorp stands out as a steady compounder that combines reliable earnings growth with meaningful income. It continues to post solid revenue gains, expand margins, and improve efficiency. It’s shifting more of its business toward fee-based revenue that reduces sensitivity to interest rates.

USB is positioned for solid EPS growth with a strong balance sheet, disciplined expense control, and added upside from the BTIG acquisition. It’s also attractively valued at 11.5 times forward earnings and has a well-covered 3.6% dividend yield. As such, USB offers the potential for total returns in the low to mid-teens if growth and modest multiple expansion play out. For these reasons, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on USB.

data.title: Whale's Tracking - Hotspots And Hedging
data.description:
As expectations of a “liquidity expansion stall” harden into consensus, markets are shifting from broad beta to sharp "cross-sectional dispersion" and "hotspot trading". This has pushed single-stock implied volatility well above index levels and, on paper, improved the carry available in dispersion strategies. In Europe, however, tighter liquidity is closer to a known quantity, and single-name liquidity is thinner, keeping implied volatility elevated for longer; meanwhile, the rapid growth of weekly and daily options amplifies dealer-hedging feedback loops, and crypto may see a similar pattern in BTC (BTC-USD), while ETH (ETH-USD) and most alts appear calmer largely because they have fallen out of the spotlight and liquidity has thinned.

Liquidity Stall, Hotspots Return

With “liquidity expansion stall” becoming a working consensus, pricing power tends to migrate away from macro beta and towards relative winners and losers. When incremental liquidity is no longer expanding, the market’s path to outperformance narrows: capital crowds into the strongest themes and names, while holders of profitable positions increasingly seek to lock in gains via downside hedges.

Both behaviours lift demand for single stock options. Hotspot trading raises idiosyncratic risk and widens the distribution of outcomes at the single-name level; profit protection boosts demand for puts, collars and other tail risk hedges. The typical outcome is a sustained premium in single-stock implied volatility relative to index implied volatility—an environment that appears conducive to dispersion trading, which monetises “single-name vol rich, index vol cheap” conditions.

In principle, the profit mechanism is straightforward: if single-stock implied volatility embeds more risk premium than ultimately realises—after accounting for realised volatility and correlation—then selling that rich single-name volatility against cheaper index volatility can earn carry over time.

However, the presence of a premium does not guarantee rapid convergence. In the current backdrop, there are structural reasons why single-name implied volatility can remain elevated for longer than many models imply—particularly in Europe.

First, the ECB’s shift away from liquidity expansion is closer to a deterministic path, anchoring expectations of tighter financial conditions. Second, single-name liquidity is typically poorer than index liquidity. When macro liquidity is tight and risk budgets shrink, the cost of warehousing and unwinding single-name risk rises, and inventory risk for market makers also becomes harder to absorb. Dealers and option sellers, therefore, often demand a higher implied volatility “insurance charge” to compensate for inventory and jump risks. Even after near-term event risk fades, that compensation can remain embedded in pricing.

For dispersion books, the practical implication is that returns may resemble a medium-term carry profile rather than a short-dated, low-variance arbitrage. A sudden rise in correlation during stress—often accompanied by an index volatility spike—can produce adverse mark-to-market moves before any longer-run convergence materialises. Feasibility is therefore governed as much by liquidity, correlation regimes and risk constraints as by the headline level of the single-name versus index implied volatility spread.

Weekly Options Take Control

Consistent with the current macro narrative and the trend of "hotspot trading", capital rotation across sectors and themes has become more violent, and hedging demand has broadened. The AI shock to parts of the technology complex illustrates how the market can simultaneously reprice "winners" and "losers": it can compress visibility for certain software business models while reinforcing upside concentration elsewhere.

In such conditions, investors tend to pay up for downside protection, lifting implied volatility in affected baskets (for instance, software-focused ETFs) and steepening index skew. A highly elevated 30-day put-call skew in QQQ is consistent with a market paying a premium for downside convexity while remaining more restrained on upside participation.

The more consequential shift is microstructure. Strong hedging and speculative demand have been met by a broader menu of weekly and daily options. Shorter maturities concentrate gamma exposure into tighter windows, forcing dealers to hedge more frequently and more mechanically. As a result, short-term spot moves can increasingly be shaped by hedging flows, strike "pinning" into expiries, and options-driven momentum—sometimes overwhelming fundamental news flow on a day-to-day basis.

This is not inconsistent with a liquidity stall: long-horizon risk appetite can be subdued even as short-horizon derivatives activity intensifies. The net effect is often that volatility is compressed into shorter time frames, with sharper, more flow-driven price action.

Crypto: BTC Fits the Pattern

Crypto markets show similar fault lines, determined mainly by derivatives depth and positioning. BTC, with a more mature options market and larger open interest, is more likely to exhibit equity-like feedback loops, where options positioning and dealer hedging meaningfully shape short-term price dynamics—particularly around heavily trafficked strikes.

Only five weeks into 2026, open interest in Bitcoin options in the crypto market (excluding the US stock market) has already rebounded to over 80% of the open interest for delta-1 contracts. In contrast, open interest in ETH options is less than 30% of that for delta-1 contracts. Source: Coinglass

ETH and the broader alt complex can look “better” only in a narrow sense: calmer conditions often reflect that they are no longer the hotspot, with less crowded positioning and weaker derivatives-driven reflexivity. That calm, however, comes with trade-offs. If ETH’s implied forward carry fails to exceed cash deposit rates, risk compensation is being compressed, and for many alts, liquidity has already thinned to the point where lower observed volatility can mask poorer tradability and higher impact costs.

The annualised implied forward yield for ETH has fallen to around 3%. Source: Amberdata Derivatives

In this environment, a more robust posture typically prioritises liquidity and drawdown control: higher cash buffers, less long-dated directional exposure, and only limited capital allocated to short-term tactical trades where downside is explicitly bounded. Hotspot trading may remain a recurring feature of the liquidity-stall regime. Still, the accompanying crowding, elevated option premia and fragile liquidity make risk management—not view-taking—the binding constraint.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: BorgWarner: Beyond Auto Parts (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I've said many times that when a management with a reputation for brilliance tackles a business with a reputation for bad economics, it is the reputation of the business that remains intact. - Warren Buffett

There are some sectors, in large part due to industry structure, that are inherently less attractive. Some examples include airlines, auto manufacturers, and automotive suppliers. There are some companies, however, that seem to have a special DNA, and still do deliver superior results. In the airline industry examples include Southwest (LUV) and Ryanair (RYAAY). In the automotive industry it could be argued that Toyota (TM) with its impressive efficiency, and Ferrari (RACE) with its brand and pricing power, are outliers as well. When it comes to automotive suppliers, we can think of only two companies that tend to be "decent" businesses, partly due to patents and other IP, as well as being well-run. These are Autoliv (ALV) and BorgWarner (BWA). And they are far from dream businesses. Their profit margins and growth are decent, but not spectacular. They can be decent stock ideas at the right price, as we previously noted, but it is critical to avoid overpaying.

One recent development that is changing the thesis on BorgWarner, and which the market rewarded immediately, is its expansion into the data center power market. More specifically, BorgWarner announced in its recent earnings call that it was launching a turbine generator system to help power data centers. There growing pressure for data centers to bring their own power, as costs get pushed onto ratepayers. Three things likely helped further boost investor optimism. First, it appears the opportunity is meaningful. Management is guiding for over $300 million in revenue in 2027, which would be the ramp-up year. Management hinted at a higher run rate in later years. Second, BorgWarner is not deviating too far from its core skills, as the new turbine generator leverages its automotive supply chain and know-how, with BorgWarner controlling roughly 65% of the components used. Finally, CEO Joseph Fadool hinted at similar opportunities in the future, saying that all business units are embracing the challenge of finding similar growth opportunities:

[...] business units are embracing the challenge to find additional growth opportunities through new product developments like our turbine generator system and our eXD solution. And in 2026, we expect to launch new products like our innovative battery cooling plates.

Financials

BorgWarner's revenue has shown historical cyclicality, as auto sales tend to experience significant weakness during economic downturns. During the financial crisis, and the early pandemic shock, sales declined significantly. This reflects the discretionary nature of the automotive market. Nevertheless, BorgWarner has managed a decent average quarterly year-over-year growth rate of approximately 4%. BorgWarner has recently made efficiency efforts to offset tariff and inflation effects, and has managed to bring its operating margin slightly above the historical average.

Sales remain highly correlated with light vehicle production (LVP), which has stalled in recent years. Growth has mostly come from higher dollar content in hybrid and electric vehicles (EVs). However EVs have also seen growth decelerate in the U.S. with the elimination of incentives. BorgWarner has adjusted to the shift, and despite modest organic sales, its adjusted earnings per share (EPS) and free cash flow (FCF) were materially higher in fiscal year 2025 compared to 2024.

Research & Development

BorgWarner is delivering on its R&D spending, with a large number of solutions for hybrid and EV vehicles. Now it is planning to branch toward adjacent industrial applications. The turbine generator is worth watching. Its cost should be competitive thanks to the reuse of automotive parts and designs. It was also designed to be fuel-flexible, and is expected to be more efficient than traditional diesel generators.

Continuing to improve turbochargers, and electric vehicle solutions, helped BorgWarner secure a record number of design wins. Over the past four quarters it accumulated 30 awards, including many for its eProducts. The strong momentum is likely to continue thanks to new product launches such as its battery cooling plates. We believe there might be potential to reuse some of the battery management expertise BorgWarner has developed in the EV industry for utility battery energy storage solutions (BESS).

BorgWarner is leveraging AI as well, mostly to improve efficiency in its back office and R&D efforts. Fadool shared that he estimates around 30% of the launched pilots appear highly promising:

[...] started about 2 years ago on some of the new generational AI technologies, and we start with some pilots. We had pilots in our plants. We had pilots in our back office and also some in R&D. And out of those pilots, let me estimate about 30% of them look really positive.

M&A Strategy

Another topic that generated several questions from analysts during the earnings call was the M&A strategy going forward. Management said they remain committed to focusing on potential acquisitions that make industrial sense, deliver near-term accretion, and where the price makes sense. They said they've screened a few opportunities, but they didn't meet all the criteria. They even mentioned raising the hurdles on the criteria. We interpret this as a sign that management is happy with the returns they are obtaining from internal R&D efforts, and will only pursue acquisitions where there is a very strong case that the operation would improve earnings power and technology differentiation.

Shareholder Returns

Instead of pursuing acquisitions, BorgWarner has been recently returning significant amounts of capital through share repurchases.

BorgWarner also pays a dividend, but it remains modest. The shareholder yield, which includes buybacks, is several times higher than the current dividend yield.

Future Outlook

Guidance for fiscal year 2026 is mixed, with revenue expected to experience a slight decline. Earnings were guided higher despite the revenue headwinds as productivity initiatives and reduced overhead are expected to deliver improved profit margins. Free cash flow for the year is expected at close to $1 billion, of which a meaningful part could be used for bolt-on acquisitions or continued share repurchases.

It is unlikely that shares would have spiked higher after earnings based only on the numbers and guidance alone. The real catalyst appears to be the branching out to serve new markets. In particular, opportunities where BorgWarner can leverage its core competencies in mechanical and electronics engineering. It appears that the turbine generator for data centers is the first of several new products BorgWarner is planning.

Valuation

With shares having delivered more than 90% since our last article in mid-2025, we believe a significant part of the previous undervaluation has been corrected. It could be argued that the current share price now reflects growth expectations outside the automotive segment. It is possible that without the turbine generator announcement shares would have declined after the recent earnings results.

Valuation multiples are slightly above their ten-year averages, but they remain reasonable in absolute terms. If the branching out to other industrial applications works out well, there is potential for further gains. However the risk/reward appears far more balanced. We are therefore adjusting our rating opinion to "Hold".

Risks

The main risk we see is execution risk on the branching out to industrial applications. Light vehicle production has seen low growth, which has historically impacted BorgWarner. The company is finding a solution by leveraging its expertise in higher growth applications. We believe the strategy to be sound, but it comes with added execution risks. And the higher share price reduces the previous valuation margin of safety.

Conclusion

BorgWarner took investors by surprise with a new promising product that helps reduce its dependence on the automotive market. We view this move as smart. Investors reacted very favorably to the news. The issue is that the valuation now incorporates expectations that the strategy will deliver meaningful additional revenue. At the current share price, and considering the added risks of targeting new end-markets, we believe a "Hold" opinion is more appropriate. The automotive market remains challenging, but BorgWarner is taking a bold step in search of growth, while continuing with efficiency measures to grow earnings.

data.title: Last Call For A Nice 2nd Pension, TLT Is OK, But I Like My Treasury Ladder Better
data.description:
Bonds have taken a back seat to stocks for nearly 20 years. I see serious signs that is about to end. And I'm jumping in, hand over fist. If that sounds aggressive, it is actually the opposite. But it allows me to be much more aggressive with the REST of my portfolio than I might otherwise be. Not just now, but for a long time. 15-20 years in fact, which for me will get me to 80 years old.

Now, let me unscramble that. Because the topic of bond ladders alongside an equity portfolio is so important to me. But the way bond rates are starting to move, this might be the last major article I write involving the opportunity I see in laddering bonds.

Last year I wrote an article describing one of the many strategies I use to capitalize on some of the highest US Treasury rates in a very long time. As I see it, successful long-term management of my portfolio starts with a solid "anchor." That is NOT an equity portfolio. No set of stocks can deliver the piece of mind that a bond ladder can.

Why? Because the way I do it, I'm essentially creating a second pension for myself. It is NOT income I am after. It is cash flow. Annual cash flow. A predictable amount of money, that I customize now, and which will continue to pay me each year. It is only limited by the funds I put into it, and how many bonds I can buy, given current rates, and my desired maturity levels.

Bond rates are historically high. Maybe as high as they will get for a while. That creates urgency to me.

This is what its all about to me. Unless inflation is going to skyrocket (something I can't predict but can hedge), yields for 10-30 year bonds are, to me, a great return/risk tradeoff. I don't forecast the future, but I do believe that a rate in the 4-5% range over a long time period will be a good ballast. It has been historically.

This is hardly the only way to own bonds. But it is my favorite way. I use zero coupon US Treasuries because I want to target a payout at maturity, not get income along the way.

And to address a question I get often when writing about bonds here, I chose to create my ladder in my IRA, not a taxable account. That allows me to compound the discounted amounts I pay (versus what they will mature for) and not pay tax on them until later.

Inflation is my enemy here. But I have a solution for that. I hedge rising rates. I wrote last time about the ProShares Short 20+ Yr Treasury ETF (TBF), which shorts long-term bonds. I think so highly of it that after having used it many times throughout the years, I included it as one of 10 ETFs I include in my most robust ETF portfolio.

TLT: the ETF few understand and I don't know why

Let's take another look at the bond ladder, then discuss why the "default" investment for owning long-term bonds and profiting from falling interest rates is ProShares Short 20+ Yr Treasury ETF (TLT), the $45 billion behemoth that has produced more heartache in dollar terms than almost any ETF I can think of. Not because it is a bad investment. Because many investors don't understand it, and don't understand bonds. That's why I am writing about them once more. Because I think the opportunity could turn out to be generational. But once it is gone, it could be years before it returns again.

Here's what I mean about TLT. See that asset surge in 2022 and 2023? That was investors either trying to buy the dip in bond prices (see the upper part of the chart, TLT's price), or it was some very good marketing. Wall Street pundits totally underestimated how high long-term rates would go.

And since they exist primarily to get investors to buy stuff, the call for buying bonds kept going and going. As TLT, including its dividend payment, fell from $125 to $75, a 40% drop. That's some dip to try to buy!

TLT is a very straightforward ETF. Its holdings look like this. Except there are many pages like this. Just about every US Treasury bond maturing from 2044-2056 is in here.

I use TLT as a supplement to my bond ladders when I believe rates are likely to fall. Not all rates, just long-term rates. And it has a liquid options market, so buying puts and calls on it is part of my ongoing active management of what is, at the core, a set of laddered, zero coupon bonds, as we'll see below.

I also use TBF, which shorts bonds, and TLTW, which adds some return via covered call writing on TLT. This is not for everyone. Frankly, if I were starting a fund again (I ran three in my career), I might just do one replicating what I do here. Specifically:

1. Bond ladder as the core - zero coupons to get more yield and set up a dedicated future cash flow. The closest thing to certainty one gets in public market investing.

2. Monitor long rates as I do actively, and use the aforementioned tools in combination to be "ready for anything."

Falling rates do to market panic? The zeroes appreciate nicely, and I might even sell some off to cash in some profits. Those can be seriously high double-digit percentage gains. Think of it this way: for every 1% drop in the yield on 20-year bonds, I estimate I'll make about 15% profit on my bond portfolio. Did I mention this is the biggest part of my portfolio? Yes, a career "hedged equity" guy, for his own retirement, has more of his money in laddered zero coupons bonds than anything else.

Why? Because the ladder allows me to lock in about a 4.5% rate or higher. I want to do better than that overall, but if a lot of my money is set at that return range, I can take some bigger chances elsewhere (my equity, options, ETF and trading portfolios). And I do.

To put some basic numbers to it, let's say you had 60% of your portfolio in a ladder yielding 4.5%. That contributes 0.6 x 4.5% = 2.7% to your return. If your 40% in equities loses 5% a year, long term, you still end up ahead in nominal terms. But if having the bonds allows you to pursue, say a 12% long-term equity return whereas in retirement you might otherwise not, that contributes another 4.8% to your total return (0.4 x 12%).

Add up the two parts and that's a 7.5% return. With very low risk. In layman's terms, that's how I'm managing my own retirement.

I'll use this chart above to answer two questions I anticipate. One is why not just "TLT and chill?" Because I don't want to endure a 30% total return decline in a 12-month period. That's what will happen if rates spike again.

And when that happens, I am glad I learned how to deploy TBF, and the options on TLT. You see above how TBF and TLT work opposite each other. I know that not everyone wants to track bond yields and analyze their charts like I do. But even if you take a small TBF or TLT put option position versus the bond ladder, those sudden upward shifts in rates are somewhat neutralized. Again, this is how I do it. I'm discussing it here so that every investor can decide for themselves what, if anything, they take from it. And apply it themselves.

A current look at the bonds I own in my ladder

Now, to the ladder. This is the shorter-term portion of a ladder that extends further out in another part of my portfolio. I just plugged in $10,000 face/maturity value as a simple example. Then applied last Friday's closing prices. But these are the bonds I own in this part of my ladder.

The bottom line of the ladder is that the $93,000 shown up there will produce $10,000 a year in cash flow at maturity. Every year for 15 years, from 2030 to 2044. That's the CONCEPT. I welcome your questions, as I realize this is not standard fare on this equity-laden platform.

Last point for now: rates might finally be making their move

Now, the kicker. Why am I writing about all of this now? Because I think time is getting shorter here. The bond market has been waffling in both directions. And frankly, I'm less concerned about the reasons. The direction of rates is all I care about. So I can respond with either a profit watch on the ladder, or a hedge on higher rates. This is a passive portfolio but with active add-ons as described above, when necessary.

This is an encouraging sign to me. It is an indication that TLT and thus longer term bonds, are hinting at finally choosing a direction. And if this follows through as it appears to be, that direction is up for prices, down for yields. Good for my bond ladder, and less hedging needed, if any.

That allows me to try to pile on with TLTW, TLT calls, or TLT itself. Or, just enjoy what I've seen recently, which is my "fixed return" bond ladder give me another choice: if it keeps rallying as it has been recently, I am very willing to cash some of it in. Because that essentially would allow me to pull forward a lot of future returns, that I would have had to wait years for, as the bond proceeded toward their long-term maturities.

Market Overview

The fourth quarter was a fitting coda to 2025. It featured many of the ingredients that were in play over the year. Markets generally set records; there was intense scrutiny on artificial intelligence (AI), the VIX volatility index was lackluster, but gold was bright; fiscal and monetary policy saw major developments; trade talks between China and the United States took another turn; and the consumer displayed resilience but also bifurcation. A key support for the markets came from higher than expected corporate earnings. Capital expenditures (capex) levels on AI remained elevated, providing ammunition for both bulls and bears. A data vacuum caused by the longest government shutdown in history added to the uncertainty for investors. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered 50 basis points (bps) of interest rate cuts over the quarter, but the larger story was the lack of consensus among Fed voters and the choice of the next chair. The United States and China agreed to lower tariffs and extend their trade truce for a year.

The 2.4% rise in the Russell 1000® Index (2.6% for the S&P 500 Index) masked divergence across styles as the Russell 1000® Value Index jumped 3.8% but the Russell 1000® Growth Index rose 1.1%. Within the Russell 1000 Index, healthcare was the best-performing sector by far, aided by the U.S. government reaching deals with major pharmaceutical companies. Stocks in the information technology and communication services sectors advanced after investors weighed confidence in the monetization of the AI evolution against worries over the outsized capital expenditure plans and the circularity of financing and business contracts in the ecosystem. Sector performance was a mixed bag with 3 of the 11 sectors posting declines over the quarter.

Small-caps in the United States underperformed large-caps, albeit marginally, with the Russell 2000® Index advancing 2.2%. As in large caps, value beat growth.

In line with the full-year pattern, both non-U.S. developed markets and emerging markets outperformed the U.S. market.

European equities celebrated improving earnings revisions. Easing trade tensions provided another boost as did optimism on AI efficiency gains and rising investor confidence. Stocks in Japan gained from AI themes and the new prime minister’s expansionary fiscal policy but were pressured by fears of interest rate hikes. The Canadian market posted strong returns as investors rewarded commodity companies and an easier monetary policy.

Chinese equities declined on signs of an economic slowdown such as weakness in retail sales and fixed-asset investment. Conversely, the Korean market was a standout performer, surging on AI-related tech themes and optimism on corporate governance reforms.
