Portfolio Review

At the start of the quarter, as compared to its MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) ex-USA Index benchmark, the Carillon ClariVest International Stock Fund was most overweight to the information technology and utilities sectors and most underweight to energy and consumer discretionary. By the end of the quarter, the portfolio was most overweight to the information technology and healthcare sectors and most underweight to energy and industrials. Regarding countries, at the start of the quarter, the portfolio was most overweight to the United Kingdom and Japan and was most underweight to Sweden and India. By the end of the quarter, the portfolio remained most overweight to Japan and the United Kingdom and most underweight to Sweden and India. Within the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index, information technology and materials performed best while communication services and consumer discretionary lagged. In terms of countries, South Korea and Chile were the strongest, while Saudi Arabia and China lagged.

As determined by sectors, stock selection contributed the most while allocation was also positive. Stock selection was strongest within healthcare and financials and weakest within industrials. An overweight to information technology and an underweight to communication services contributed to performance while underweights to financials and materials detracted. With regard to countries, stock selection contributed the most, though allocation was also positive. Overweights to South Korea and Taiwan contributed to performance while an underweight to South Africa and an overweight to Japan detracted from performance. Stock selection was strongest within Japan and South Korea and weakest within Taiwan and the United Kingdom.

Top securities

SK Hynix is a South Korea-based manufacturer of semiconductor memory. Results beat estimates amid strong demand and tight supply of high-bandwidth memory, with all dynamic random access memory (DRAM) and NAND memory supply fully booked for 2026.

Sumitomo Electric Industries manufactures electric wires, cables, and related equipment. The Japanese company reported strong results that beat estimates and raised guidance on robust demand for automotive, environmental, energy, and AI-related products. It also benefited from a lower than expected impact from U.S. tariffs.

Teva Pharmaceutical Industries is a global pharmaceutical company based in Israel. Results beat expectations with stronger margins and significantly strong performance in the United States by one of its key drugs. Its generics segment also was a main driver.

Samsung Electronics, a South Korea-based consumer and industrial electronics manufacturer, reported its biggest quarterly profit in more than three years, driven by healthy AI chip demand and a sharp recovery in high-bandwidth memory pricing.

Taiwan Semiconductor is the world’s largest semiconductor chip manufacturer. The company is expected to benefit from the AI revolution as hyperscalers build out data centers.

Bottom securities

Alibaba Group Holding, a China-based online retailer, reported better than expected sales, but shares fell due to concerns about slowing consumer spending in China and rising competition in e-commerce.

3i Group is a UK-based private equity and investment company. Its largest holding, Action, reported slower sales growth than expected, driven by weak consumer spending, especially in France.

Tencent Holdings is an investment holding company headquartered in China. Results beat expectations, but the stock declined as investors worried about rising competition, heavy spending on AI, and slowing consumer demand.

Prosus, a Netherlands-based global consumer internet group, reported results viewed as in line with expectations, but the share price slipped due to a lower buyback outlook and a lack of updated guidance.

ASUSTeK Computer, based in Taiwan, manufactures and sells computers, communication, and consumer electronics. The company reported better than expected personal computer and AI server revenues, but the stock fell amid concerns of rising memory costs and tepid hardware demand.

Outlook

The U.S. market is being buoyed by many themes, including AI-related capex, expected efficiency gains from generative AI, measures from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act taking effect, anticipated interest rate cuts, deregulation, and rising mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity. There are concerns bubbling under the surface on valuations, the level and structure of the capex in AI, and policy uncertainty. Outside the United States, several countries took fiscal, monetary, or corporate reform actions to mitigate the impact of U.S. tariffs and policies. Those actions, aided by favorable relative valuations, helped non-U.S. markets deliver higher performance last year, and we expect them to continue to provide support going into 2026.

We believe underappreciated growth opportunities are sprinkled among the uncertainties around the world. A complex geopolitical environment, concentration risks, rising debt, and fiscal deficits are concerns. We favor looking through the noise, emphasizing diversification, and staying focused on the underlying company-level fundamentals.

Risk Considerations:

International investing presents specific risks, such as currency fluctuations, differences in financial accounting standards, and potential political and economic instability. These risks are further accentuated in emerging market countries, where risks can also include possible economic dependency on revenues from particular commodities or on international aid or development assistance, currency transfer restrictions, and liquidity risks related to lower trading volumes.

Growth companies are expected to increase their earnings at a certain rate. When these expectations are not met, investors may punish the stocks excessively, even if earnings showed an absolute increase. Growth company stocks also typically lack the dividend yield that can cushion stock prices in market downturns. The companies engaged in the technology industry are subject to fierce competition and their products and services may be subject to rapid obsolescence. The values of these companies tend to fluctuate sharply.

Groups of stocks, such as value and growth, go in and out of favor, which may cause certain funds to underperform other equity funds.

Investments in the securities of other investment companies, including money market funds and exchange-traded funds (“ETFs”) (which may, in turn invest in equities, bonds, and other financial vehicles), may involve duplication of advisory fees and certain other expenses.

Quantitative risk involves the dependence on proprietary quantitative tools for security selection which may not be predictive of a security’s value.

Securities in the Carillon ClariVest International Stock Fund are typically selected from investment universes consisting of companies economically tied to, and principally traded in, countries outside of the United States.

Past performance is not indicative of future results and investing involves risk, including the risk of loss. All information as of Dec. 31, 2025. Opinions expressed are the current opinions as of the date appearing in this material only. This material should not be construed as research or investment advice. No part of this material may, without Carillon Tower Advisers’ prior written consent, be copied, photocopied, or duplicated in any form, by any means.

The information provided should not be construed as a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. The data is shown for informational purposes only and is not indicative of future portfolio characteristics or returns. Portfolio holdings are not stagnant and may change over time without prior notice.

Investors may not make direct investments into any index.

Definitions

Artificial intelligence (AI) – A technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is a form of artificial intelligence that can create new content that includes text, audio, code, video, and images. A full-stack generative AI strategy combines different technologies to create an efficient and interconnected system to generate content, automate tasks, and perform other AI functions.

Basis points (bps) – Measurements used in discussions of interest rates and other percentages in finance. One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

Beat – When a company’s reported earnings or other business results exceed or are better than the expectations of analysts and others who follow the company’s stock.

Bifurcation / bifurcated consumer base – A trend in consumer spending patterns created by two distinct segments of consumers: A more affluent and free-spending group, and a less affluent and more cost-conscious group.

Capital expenditures, or capex, are monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain physical assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

Circularity – A condition in an industry with many closely related players that may feature companies investing in products or services that are mainly used by other companies in the industry, revenue or demand loops where growth is driven by activity within the industry ecosystem, or a lack of diversification in products or services with utility to companies or consumers outside the industry itself.

Clearing event – An event or development that has the potential to eliminate, mitigate, or minimize specific challenges in an area of business.

Concentration risk – The hazards posed to individual investments or market stability by excessive concentration of returns or other characteristics in certain areas.

Dynamic random access memory (DRAM) – A type of semiconductor memory used for the data or program code needed by a computer processor to function.

Fiscal deficit spending – Government spending over a specific period of time that exceeds the revenues that the government takes in for the same period.

Fiscal policy – The tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country’s economy.

Growth investing – A investing strategy that focuses on companies expected to grow at an above-average rate compared to their industry or the market.

Guidance – Statements from the managers of publicly traded companies that indicate whether they expect to realize near-term profits or losses and why.

Hyperscalers – The largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

Macroeconomic – Relating to the branch of economics that focuses on the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Market capitalization, market cap – The total dollar market value of a company’s outstanding shares of stock.

Monetary policy – The decisions made by central banks to raise or lower benchmark interest rates or otherwise tighten or loosen credit to influence an economy’s growth, inflation, or employment levels.

NAND memory – A type of flash computer memory that reduces erase and write times lower compared to hard drives and offers more storage density at lower cost. Proponents see NAND technology improving performance, capacity, and power efficiency of solid state drives. The term NAND comes from a logic gate known as the NAND gate (short for NOT-AND).

One Big Beautiful Bill Act – An act passed and signed into law in July 2025 that raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, and changed a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, making changes to health insurance legislation, phasing out or reducing credits for clean energy production or use, and removing tax benefits for illegal immigrants.

Operating margin – How much profit a company makes on a dollar of sales after paying production costs such as wages and raw materials, but before paying interest or tax. It is calculated by dividing a company’s operating income by its net sales.

Overweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Underweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Value investing – An investment strategy that involves picking stocks that appear to be trading for less than their intrinsic or book value.

VIX, or Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index – A real-time market index that represents the market’s expectation of 30-day forward-looking volatility. Derived from the price inputs of the S&P 500 index options, it provides a measure of market risk and investors’ sentiments.

Indices

Prior to March 1, 2023, the fund’s benchmark index was the MSCI EAFE (Europe, Australasia, and the Far East) Net Index, an index that measures the performance of large- and mid-cap companies across 21 developed markets countries, excluding the United States and Canada. The fund changed its primary benchmark to the MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) ex-USA Index, a float-adjusted market capitalization index that measures the performance of large- and mid-cap companies in developed and emerging market countries excluding the United States, because it more accurately reflects the fund’s investment strategy.

Russell 1000® Index – Measures the performance of the 1,000 largest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 93% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000® Index.

Russell 1000® Growth Index – Measures a growth-oriented subset of the Russell 1000® Index, which tracks approximately 1,000 of the large-sized capitalization companies in the United States equities market.

Russell 1000® Value Index – Measures a value-oriented subset of the Russell 1000® Index, which tracks approximately 1,000 of the large-sized capitalization companies in the U.S. equities market. Russell 2000.

Russell 2000® Index – Measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index, which represents about 7% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000 Index.

S&P 500 Index – Measures change in stock market conditions based on the average performance of 500 widely held common stocks. It is a market-weighted index calculated on a total return basis with dividends reinvested. The S&P 500 represents approximately 80% of the investable U.S. equity market.

data.title: A Three-Pronged Major Market Rotation Is Just Beginning
data.description:
Ever since early 2023, when ChatGPT went mainstream and Nvidia (NVDA) delivered blowout guidance, the S&P 500 (SPY) has been in a seemingly irreversible upward climb, and the NASDAQ (QQQ) has surged even more as investors have piled into AI-related tech and software stocks due to an expectation that AI would dominate the global economy in the coming years and decades.

Meanwhile, persistently high inflation rates and, with it, persistently high long-term interest rates have hurt real asset companies, especially in the REIT (VNQ) and the broader high-yield investing universe (DIV), as these stocks floundered from the beginning of 2022 through the end of 2025.

However, due to three major factors, there is a significant rotation underway in which, so far this year, software companies (IGV) and increasingly AI leaders at large (XLK) are seeing their stock prices decline, while real asset companies or real asset investments are ascending.

In this article, I will detail what these three reasons are and why I think we are just in the early stages of this market rotation.

AI Is Eating Software Moats

One big reason is that the rise in AI, especially with recent applications of it developed by companies like Anthropic and OpenAI, has caused the market to have serious concerns about the sustainability of many digital-first businesses and, in particular, software companies. The concern is that, with AI becoming an extremely talented software engineer, what used to require immense sums of capital and skilled software engineering labor can now be done in a short period of time by a very small team of humans with the aid of AI coding tools. As a result, the moats around many of these software businesses are deemed to be much narrower than previously thought or, in some cases, even non-existent.

While some are claiming that many software companies will even possibly go extinct, and you can see this in the rapid deterioration of stock prices for many of these companies, I personally think this is unlikely, especially for the more well-established ones that have high switching costs. However, their profit margins are likely to shrink given the fact that very similar software can be created at a low cost, which will likely lead to an abundance of competition and will force them to reduce their profit margins and likely reduce demand for their products. While the software companies with deeply embedded partnerships with companies will likely retain many of those, they will also probably have to make some concessions on the price front and potentially even the volume front to keep that business in place.

Therefore, no matter how you slice and dice it, I think that it is highly likely that a large number of software companies will experience permanent impairment to their valuation relative to where it was before the rise of high-quality AI coding tools. What this means is that investors are no longer seeing AI companies and software companies as the safe havens that they once were, as they, in many cases, are becoming the victims of their own success, with the rapid rise of AI leveling the playing field and allowing new entrants into industries that they previously dominated.

As a result, investors are rotating capital out of these businesses that they previously viewed as safe, growth-at-a-reasonable-cost, growth-at-a-reasonable-price investments, and instead are putting that capital into AI-resistant and AI-proof industries. Perhaps the most AI-proof and AI-resistant of all are real asset industries because, whether it be land, energy, or natural resources and commodities, these are not things that can be created out of thin air by AI. Instead, they will become the new primary constraints of economic productivity since intelligence and manpower will likely not be in the not-too-distant future due to the wide proliferation of low-cost to even free AI tools.

De-Dollarization Is Accelerating Faster Than Most Investors Realize

The second big reason why this market shift is taking place is because of the ongoing and arguably even accelerating de-dollarization trend. This was already seen several years ago with the skyrocketing price of gold (GLD) that then picked up steam with silver (SLV), platinum (PPLT), and palladium (PALL) also soaring last year, and is now spreading across the broader real asset space.

Global de-dollarization is being fueled in large part by persistent budget deficits in the United States, with no end in sight. Additionally, the harsh sanctions placed on Russia following its invasion of Ukraine have caused many other would-be adversaries of the United States, and in particular China, to reconsider how much exposure they have to dollars in their reserves. Finally, the remaking of the global order in the wake of President Trump's tariff policy and diplomatic negotiation styles is also causing global institutions to consider diversifying away from the dollar.

These forces are all combining to prompt global institutions, which until recently were content to hoard dollars in the form of Treasuries (TLT) and other dollar-denominated paper assets, to de-dollarize and de-risk away from the dollar in the United States. Instead, they are putting those dollars into real assets that can withstand inflation and a potential dollar crisis in the future. Not only that, but these assets, in some cases, are not as easy to seize and sanction. Obviously, gold and silver and other metals and commodities fit into that category, but also, increasingly, energy commodities and stocks (XLE) are getting a boost, as witnessed by the soaring price of many energy companies (XOM)(CVX).

Infrastructure (UTF), utilities (XLU), and real estate have also been getting a boost from the market recently, and I expect that trend to continue moving forward as de-dollarization continues.

Meanwhile, AI and software companies are not real assets and, therefore, are not inherently likely to soar as inflation picks up, especially when considering the aforementioned point that AI will likely weaken their moats and, as a result, eat into their profit margins. This is why megacap AI and software powerhouses like Microsoft (MSFT) and Palantir (PLTR) have been declining sharply recently:

$700 Billion In AI CapEx: Who Really Wins?

Finally, this rotation is also being driven by the combination of lofty valuations in the mega-cap technology and AI sectors relative to pretty cheap valuations in many real asset sectors. Moreover, especially in the mega-cap tech space, the enormous amounts of money, to the tune of about $600 to $700 billion expected this year alone as mega-cap tech stocks are sinking into AI-related CapEx, are giving many in the market pause about the returns they can expect on new dollars being deployed at current valuations. That is because the vast amount of money that's being sunk into AI-related infrastructure, including data centers and energy required to power those data centers, has an uncertain return profile, and the relatively low amount of revenue currently being generated from AI products on a comparative basis means that there is going to have to be an enormous ramp-up in AI-related revenue for these types of numbers to make any sort of sense. Additionally, there is significant innovation risk because, while AI is advancing rapidly in some areas, it still has a long way to go, especially in the area of agentic AI and robotics, before it can truly manifest the sort of economic impact that is currently being priced into many AI-related stocks.

Not only that, but many real asset businesses, especially infrastructure and energy companies, are positioned to profit significantly from AI CapEx spending, whether it be midstream (AMLP) giants like Enbridge (ENB), TC Energy (TRP), Kinder Morgan (KMI), The Williams Companies (WMB), and others, or energy power producers like Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra Corp. (VST), or even more diversified AI infrastructure plays like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), and other data center and infrastructure plays. Therefore, it would seem to be a much safer bet to put money into the picks and shovels plays, especially given that it can be done at reasonable valuations and, in some cases, very attractive dividend yields at the moment, rather than betting on high-priced AI innovators and CapEx investors with a much more uncertain return profile.

Investor Takeaway

Simply buying the S&P 500 and Nasdaq has been a tremendously profitable investing strategy for the past several years. However, in my view, this run has likely come to an end as the market is now rotating capital out of the AI and tech plays that have led those industries higher and instead is pouring into real asset businesses.

With AI posing as a big threat to tech company moats, de-dollarization pushing global institutions out of treasuries and into real assets, massive AI capex putting tech powerhouses increasingly at risk while providing a strong growth catalyst for some real asset businesses, and the large valuation gap between tech and real asset businesses beginning to close, the ongoing market rotation appears likely to continue for the foreseeable future.

For investors who want to take advantage of this ongoing market rotation, de-risk their portfolios in the face of AI disruption, and lock in attractive and growing passive income, identifying and buying undervalued, high-quality, and high-yielding real asset investments looks like a great buying opportunity right now. At High Yield Investor, we have leaned heavily into this trend with a 7%+ yielding portfolio that, over the past five years, has delivered significant total return outperformance with below-market beta. If this ongoing rotation continues as we expect, we believe owners of these types of real asset stocks will likely enjoy further outperformance in the coming years.

data.title: Nebius: The Devil Is In The Details
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) has delivered a strong Q4 earnings report that continue to support momentum through 2026, given resilient AI compute demand and pricing dynamics. Specifically, the company’s full year annual recurring revenue (“ARR”) reached $1.2 billion exiting 2025, outpacing the high-end of its earlier guidance. This continues to reinforce support for Nebius’ maintained ARR guidance for 2026 at $7 billion to $9 billion, which implies another three-fold jump in 2027 revenue. The results underscore extended visibility into Nebius’ growth trajectory and market share gains, backed by a combination of improving cloud contract duration, volume and pricing dynamics. Nebius’ execution is further corroborated by its narrowing operating and adjusted EBITDA losses.

However, a deeper dive into Nebius’ latest fundamental composition continues to cast uncertainties to its earnings inflection and free cash flow (“FCF”) accretion prospects. As discussed in my previous coverage on Nebius, underlying fundamentals remain prone to volatile component cost pressures, while implications from the adverse audit opinion on its internal control for financial reporting (“ICFR”) environment remain a risk overhang. This is especially relevant to Nebius, given the durability of the AI investment thesis has become increasingly hinged on ROI visibility and quality to compensate for rising capital intensity. Although Nebius’ execution consistency in expanding ARR is backed by strong demand, its pathway to a scalable earnings inflection and capital self-sufficiency remains uncertain. This is further pressured by its accelerating capex trajectory ahead, which is expected to jump almost five-fold to as much as $20 billion this year.

At 7.3x NTM sales and 18.4x NTM EBITDA, Nebius is now trading near multiples exhibited across its hyperscaler counterparts like Microsoft (MSFT), which are inherently better de-risked given proven profitability and capital self-sufficiency at scale. Although Nebius’ multi-fold growth outlook clearly outpaces that of Big Tech peers trading at comparable multiples, the stock’s current valuation premium continues to embed expectations for a sustained profitability inflection that remains unproven. As a result, limited visibility into its earnings and FCF accretion trajectory is likely to remain a persistent multiple compression risk, challenging the stock’s upside potential from current levels.

ROI Inflection Remains Uncertain

Nebius’ headline earnings metrics corroborate strong execution in 2025. The company’s revenues accelerated 547% y/y to $227.7 million in Q4, contributing significantly to the 355% y/y increase in full year 2025 sales. The continuity of this momentum was corroborated by $1.2 billion in ARR exiting 2025, with 2026 revenue guided at almost 3x that metric between $3 billion and $3.4 billion. The company also reported narrowing adjusted EBITDA loss, as full year 2025 operating margins show improvement.

Looking ahead, management expects Nebius to exit 2026 with ARR between $7 billion and $9 billion. If the approximately three-fold ARR-to-revenue conversion implied by 2026 guidance holds into 2027, and capacity and demand continues to scale in lock-step, then this could translate to as much as $27 billion in revenue next year. This would imply a 7x revenue step-up from 2026, underscoring potential scale benefits ahead.

However, a deeper dive into Nebius’ cost functions continue to shed uncertainties on its ability to scale operations, limiting visibility into its earnings inflection outlook and AI returns prospects.

Specifically, the company’s operating losses grew 80% sequentially in Q4, far outpacing the 56% q/q revenue growth observed over the same period. This was primarily driven by higher sales and depreciation costs, which jumped 79% and 83% q/q, respectively. Although Nebius continues to sell out before capacity even comes online, underscoring a robust demand environment, supported by expanding contract durations that underscore growth visibility, its accelerating SG&A spend highlights rising customer acquisition costs. The anticipated extension to its GPU asset depreciation schedule from four years to five years starting Q1 is also unlikely to fully reverse the sizable jump in recent depreciation and amortization expense, especially as capital investments intensify.

Specifically, management guides $16 billion to $20 billion in capex this year, which represents a more than four-fold jump from $4 billion deployed in 2025. Although headline guidance suggests up to 536% y/y revenue growth in 2026 on 4x capex expansion, which implies improving capital efficiency, it isn’t conclusive of sustained ROI expansion.

Nebius’ AI returns outlook remains dictated by its ability to scale capacity expansion and utilization, which also hinges on the timing of contract fulfilment and subsequent revenue recognition. This accordingly exposes it to ongoing fluctuations in external cost factors across the broader AI value chain, spanning both infrastructure and power cost implications, in particular.

As discussed in the previous coverage, power constraints remain an intensifying bottleneck on AI developments. Although Nebius has been proactive in power procurement, raising its contracted capacity target from 2GW previously to 3GW by year-end, its rapid expansion roadmap remains prone to energy cost inflation risks driven by broader industry supply constraints.

The contracted power doesn’t fully insulate Nebius’ unit economics from, given the timing lag in converting to available data center capacity. Specifically, only about 800MW to 1GW of the contracted power is expected to be operational and revenue-generating by year-end. This effectively keeps a meaningful portion of Nebius’ procured energy exposed to risks of pricing reset as deployment progresses, amplified by rising costs of converting the contracted supply into operational capacity amid strained utilities and data center supply chains. The persistent uncertainties to scaling its core AI cloud unit economics also risks being further exacerbated by rising sales costs, as discussed in the earlier section.

Management’ expectation for the majority of expanded capacity this year to come online in 2H26 also implies a meaningful lag between capex deployment and revenue recognition. The timing gap could risk furthering uncertainty to Nebius’ near-term AI returns trajectory – similar to what’s been recently observed at Microsoft. Specifically, Microsoft’s been a key beneficiary of secular AI opportunities, especially in the core infrastructure layer. However, this accelerating demand environment continues to be bottlenecked by power and compute supply availability. In Microsoft’s case, capex has been repeatedly on the rise in recent quarters to bring additional capacity online. Yet a commensurate uplift to near-term growth remains absent, harbingering AI ROI compression risks. And for Nebius, similar exposure could be a larger consequential downside risk, given its persistent losses and negative FCF leave less room for absorbing risks of related cost overruns as new capacity buildout scales.

Operating Cash Flow Visibility Could Be Temporary

Nebius’ operating cash flows have turned positive in Q4, driven primarily by upfront cash payments received on long-term customer commitments. And management expects the dynamic to persist throughout the remainder of the year:

We ended the year with $3.7 billion in cash and cash equivalents and generated $834 million in operating cash flow in Q4, which was primarily comprised of upfront payments from our long-term agreements. These early cash flows will continue over the course of the year as we execute against these commitments, providing us with significant visibility into future cash flow.

Source: Nebius Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

This is corroborated by management’s expectation for consistent execution on several large contracts with hyperscalers, including contracted capacity deployment for Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft which will continue through 2026 as planned.

The pulled forward cash receipts are constructive for Nebius’ rising capital intensity, with capex expected to rise by more than 4x this year to as much as $20 billion, which management intends to fund up to 60% with internal resources. The improved near-term cash flows could also improve Nebius’ access to external financing, which is relevant given the company’s intentions to tap into corporate debt and asset-backed financing to fund its accelerating growth investments.

However, the near-term operating cash flow inflection is driven primarily by working capital changes stemming from higher deferred revenue and customer prepayments rather than earnings accretion at scale. This suggests continued uncertainties to the sustainability of Nebius’ operating cash flow generation. The durability of Nebius’ timing-driven cash flow profile is further undermined by its widening adjusted net loss, as operating leverage remains pressured by increasing sales costs while infrastructure and product development spend stays elevated amid rapid AI advancement cycles.

Taken together, the front-loaded operating cash inflow has yet to validate scalability in Nebius’ underlying unit economics. Without scale, the current pace of operating cash generation could lack durability – revenue might continue to accelerate as contracted performance obligations are fulfilled, but the ensuing cash impact remains at risk of stalling as customer payments have been largely pulled forward. While management’s expectation for sustained positive operating cash flow in 2026 could help de-risk near-term funding needs amid the impending step-up in both operating spend and capex, it’s yet to prove that Nebius’ AI investment returns are structurally inflecting on a cash basis. This disconnect effectively undermines the growth and earnings expectations currently embedded in Nebius’ valuation premium, representing a looming risk overhang that remains underappreciated as the stock trades near multiples of its better de-risked hyperscaler peers.

Final Thoughts

Admittedly, Nebius’ Q4 results reinforce visibility into its forward growth trajectory. Its better-than-expected full year ARR of $1.2 billion exiting 2025 effectively lends credibility to management’s target of $7 billion to $9 billion ARR by the end of the current year. This essentially extends Nebius’ prospective runway for multi-fold revenue growth and market share gains despite intensifying industry competition, reinforced by longer contract duration and resilient pricing dynamics on both current and next-generation GPUs.

However, the AI trade debate is rapidly shifting from “growth at all costs” to “scalable returns,” as investors increasingly demand ROI visibility amid an intensifying capex cycle. With Nebius’ capex set to increase by almost five-fold to $16 billion to $20 billion this year, the stock’s premium increasingly hinges on its ability to convert ARR momentum into sustained ROI expansion and FCF accretion that can tangibly fund continued growth investments. This is particularly relevant, given Nebius’ ability to scale unit economics remains unproven despite visibility into a protracted multi-fold revenue growth trajectory. This is evidenced by Q4’s reacceleration in operating expenses, even as revenue grew by 547%.

And while the positive inflection in operating cash flows is favorable to near-term funding optics, Nebius has yet to establish a sustainable cash generation profile rooted in structural earnings accretion. Coupled with limited visibility into Nebius’ ICFR remediation until its 2025 20-F is filed, the adverse audit opinion represents an incremental risk premium that remains underappreciated. Taken together, the durability of Nebius’ current valuation remains prone to trade-offs, especially in a market that’s become less forgiving of companies with uncertain AI return profiles.

data.title: Wave Life Sciences: WVE-006 Now In Focus, Navigating Opportunity And Risk
data.description:
Thesis: A closer look at WVE-006

Wave Life Sciences’ (WVE) investor confidence has been pretty tightly linked to their pipeline optionality, particularly the early clinical promise we saw in WVE-007 for obesity. Towards the end of last year, we saw strong Phase 1 INLIGHT data, with robust and durable Activin E reductions. There was also potential for fat loss and metabolic improvements. It made for quite a good viewing and boosted investor enthusiasm to say the least. As you know, it drove the stock from around $7.50 all the way up to about $21, which I felt was a reflection of outsized expectations. The potential is there, no doubt; however, we are still quite early in the overall picture, and since then, the share price has normalised to the $12 range. The stock also seems to be on a downward slope with investors coming to terms with both the inherent clinical and regulatory risk in early-stage biotech and the market’s reassessment of near-term catalysts. We have to keep in mind that Wave has a pretty diverse pipeline and that, despite WVE-007 showing good promise, negative sentiment among other candidates can cut the gains we saw from WVE-007.

So whilst WVE-007 is showing meaningful promise as a non-GLP-1 approach to obesity, the more immediate focus, I think, shifts to WVE-006. It's a GalNAc-conjugated RNA editing candidate for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. The recent return of full rights from GSK, I feel, should be viewed as rather concerning. Both in terms of opportunity and risk. Wave now has full control over regulatory strategy, including a potential accelerated approval pathway, but it also assumes all operational, manufacturing, and commercialisation responsibilities, which isn't going to be easy, as I'll explain. So, how the company navigates this transition and the upcoming RestorAATion-2 multidose data release will most likely be a very important near-term indicator of investor confidence. I do feel, however, that there is more upside to Wave stock in the longer term, but with the current trend, I think that share price may continue to decline, which gives me cause to put a hold rating.

3Q25 recap

Last earnings Wave reported a net loss of about $53.9 million for 3Q25, a figure which was a slight improvement from the $61.8 million loss we saw a year ago. It showed slightly better operating leverage despite a continued heavy investment in R&D. The company managed to generate $7.6 million in revenue, with research and development expenses increasing to $45.9 million. Elsewhere, general and administrative expenses jumped to $18.1 million with the company advancing multiple clinical programs. Cash and cash equivalents totaled $196.2 million as of the last quarter. In terms of operating spend, they reported a quarterly operating expense of about $64.0 million, which would imply a quarterly cash burn for the most part in line with its net loss of about $54 million. However, since last quarter, we’ve seen the company add about $72.1 million from its ATM facility and committed GSK milestones. On top of this, back in December, they raised roughly $350 million in gross proceeds from an upsized public offering. So now on a pro forma basis, we’re looking at a total available cash figure of roughly $618 million. Now, if we take the recent quarterly operating spend as a proxy, they have a capital base that supports an estimated runway of around 10 quarters, or about 2.5 years. It pushes Wave’s operational funding into 2028, which is going to be pretty important given their multiple late-stage clinical programs.

The current pipeline

As for the current pipeline, Wave’s portfolio seems increasingly diversified, with a multi-modality approach across RNAi, RNA editing, exon-skipping, and allele-selective oligonucleotide mechanisms. Now, several of their programs are in clinical development addressing both common and rare diseases. Starting with WVE-007, it’s an investigational GalNAc-siRNA targeting INHBE for obesity. So far, it has demonstrated pretty robust, dose-dependent reductions in Activin E in their ongoing INLIGHT trial. There have also been sustained effects supporting infrequent dosing with multiple upcoming data readouts through mid-2026, which we will get into.

Elsewhere, they have an alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD), WVE-006. This is a GalNAc-conjugated A-to-I RNA editing oligonucleotide (AIMer) designed to correct the pathogenic Pi*ZZ mutation we see in AATD. So far, in the RestorAATion-2 study, it has managed to achieve key treatment goals, including an impressive restoration of physiologically relevant AAT protein levels. It also managed to reduce the mutant Z-AAT, with additional cohort data also expected in early to mid-2026. Staying on the topic of AIMer, WVE-008 is being developed for PNPLA3 I148M-associated liver disease, and has been advanced as the next clinical candidate with a Clinical Trial Application planned sometime in 2026.

As for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), WVE-N531 is an exon-skipping oligonucleotide, specifically for boys amenable to exon 53 skipping. It’s shown statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements in muscle function in Phase 2 and is now on track for a New Drug Application filing in 2026. Finally, we have to mention WVE-003 for Huntington’s disease (HD), which is a first-in-class allele-selective oligonucleotide that is tipped to selectively lower mutant huntingtin (mHTT) whilst also, and very importantly, managing to preserve the wild-type HTT. The SELECT-HD data demonstrated some allele-selective target engagement and decent correlations with structural neurodegeneration. The company is heading towards a potentially registrational Phase 2/3 study following a much-anticipated IND submission by investors. So with these programs, you can see why the last equity raise was so important.

Global rights to WVE-006

As you know, a couple of weeks ago, Wave managed to regain full global rights to its AATD program WVE-006 from GlaxoSmithKline (GSK). At the time, it was a pretty strategic shift in how the asset itself would be advanced and potentially commercialised. You see, it was originally partnered with GSK to leverage its development and commercialisation infrastructure, but now the program has returned to Wave after both companies concluded that Wave is best positioned to efficiently drive late-stage development. This is the narrative we were told based on the strength of emerging clinical data. As you know, WVE-006 is a GalNAc-conjugated RNA editing oligonucleotide seen to correct the pathogenic Pi*ZZ mutation that causes AATD. So, in enabling restoration of the functional AAT protein and also reducing toxic mutant Z-AAT, the candidate can address two issues here, both the lung and liver manifestations of the disease.

Just a little bit of deeper insight on this, since I think it is quite important in relation to the candidates' differentiation in a potential future market. WVE-006 enables edits in the mutant SERPINA1 mRNA, called Pi*ZZ, toward the wild-type sequence. WVE-006 actually increases the production of properly folded, functional AAT protein that can then be secreted into the bloodstream, thereby restoring protease inhibition in the lung. It also protects against neutrophil elastase-driven tissue damage. Now, at the same time, in reducing levels of misfolded Z-AAT protein in hepatocytes, the therapy can decrease intracellular aggregation and endoplasmic reticulum stress in the liver also. So that side of things directly targets the driver of liver inflammation, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. Patients are then getting a dual effect, a boosted circulating functional AAT and also lowered toxic intracellular Z-AAT. This allows for a single therapy to mechanistically treat both the pulmonary and hepatic manifestations of AATD.

This is a limitation of a lot of current therapies, and there was a lot of investor confidence built into this fact earlier in the candidate's development. We saw encouraging data already showing physiologically meaningful AAT restoration and also mutant protein reduction. Wave now has plans to pursue an accelerated approval pathway and is set to engage directly with the FDA on a potential filing strategy, which is good to hear. We can also expect regulatory feedback mid-year. Operationally speaking, this transition now gives Wave full control over development timelines, trial design, and regulatory strategy. Now, whether this is a good or a bad thing, we are yet to find out, but it should allow Wave to retain all future economic upside rather than sharing it with a partner. I think this moves WVE-006 up a notch in the pipeline and should now be seen as more of a potential near-term value driver for the company. There’s upcoming multidose data from the RestorAATion-2 trial sometime in 1Q26 that could support registrational planning. If we see positive signals, this should set up Wave as a standalone developer of the first RNA editing therapy for AATD that actually targets the root genetic cause of the disease.

The risk in WVE-006

Now, I mentioned that this could be viewed through a positive or a negative lens earlier. We should also keep in mind that this can easily be viewed much more sceptically. It seems intuitive that Wave regaining rights to WVE-006 from GlaxoSmithKline is more likely a negative signalhere. The core assumption is that we don't typically see large pharma partners walk away from assets they believe have strong commercial or clinical potential. And no matter how positively both companies framed the press release, it seems somewhat strange, especially after the meaningful data that had been generated.

From this perspective, I think GSK’s decision to relinquish rights would suggest it may have reassessed the risk/reward profile internally. As you know, big pharma companies do this often and with far more information and expertise than we can. So they must have concluded the program is not attractive enough to justify late-stage development and commercialisation. Even if the clinical data we saw were encouraging, the argument here is that Wave now faces a much harder road. They have to take on regulatory strategy, manufacturing scale-up, payer engagement, and then global commercialisation, all of which have to be done alone and also for the first time since they don’t have any approved products on the market yet.

I would also make the argument here that complex oligonucleotide therapies are far from easy to commercialise. You see, the manufacturing and CMC are unusually complex. Oligos need highly specialised synthesis, purification, and quality control, since very small changes in chemistry/stereochemistry can actually affect their activity and safety. So for Wave, taking on this task alone and for the first time, scaling from clinical to commercial volumes won't be easy. I often see regulators scrutinise batch consistency very closely in this sense. So in that sense, I do expect the cost of goods to be higher and tech transfer riskier for Wave moving forward.

The other area I see some risk is around a potential dosing paradigm. It could be the case that it’s unfamiliar to physicians and payers. I often see oligo drugs require chronic injections, whether subcutaneous or intrathecal, and they have unique tissue targeting, such as GalNAc to the liver. They have to rely on biomarker-driven efficacy rather than immediately obvious clinical endpoints. That could complicate labelling and reimbursement a bit. This brings me to the point of patient identification. As you know, it is far is harder, especially in rare genetic diseases, to identify patients. You often need genotyping and biomarker confirmation, and in some cases, longitudinal monitoring of protein correction rather than symptom relief. That's a bit of a drag on rapid uptake. Lastly, with GSK out of the picture, I think the commercial infrastructure is going to be far more expensive relative to patient numbers for Wave. With it targeting such a small population, they’re going to need all the help they can get in the form of speciality sales, medical affairs, nurse support, and possibly market access teams. And keep in mind this is going to be spread over far fewer patients. A large pharma like GSK could have absorbed all that, but a small biotech like Wave is going to struggle to do it efficiently, in my opinion.

Valuation

Wave now trades at a market cap figure of about $2.3 billion, with an enterprise value of about $2.15 billion. It’s a reflection of a valuation that's driven almost entirely by pipeline optionality. Now, given their very minimal commercial revenue and also ongoing losses, which we can expect to continue, traditional earnings-based metrics such as price to earnings are not yet meaningful. So it makes sales and asset-based multiples more appropriate metrics. On this basis, Wave would screen as rather expensive. They hold a trailing EV to sales of about 19.7x and forward EV to sales of 53x, which are several hundred percent above healthcare sector medians. It goes to show us just how much future success is already priced into the stock. Elsewhere, the company trades at a price to book ratio of 16x, again far above sector norms, which implies investors are valuing Wave’s intellectual property and clinical pipeline well above the balance-sheet value of its assets.

Overall, I think Wave’s valuation reflects a pretty high-risk, albeit high-reward profile typical of a late clinical-stage biotech company. Those who choose to buy in at current levels will be putting their confidence in the probability of regulatory success and eventual commercialisation. As for volume, Wave currently trades with an average daily trading volume in the low millions. The recent 20‑session averages have seen numbers around 2.2 to 4.2 million shares, so there is reasonably solid liquidity here for a clinical‑stage biotech name.

Looking ahead

Next, Wave seems poised for several near-term catalysts. WVE-006 data from the multidose cohort of the RestorAATion-2 trial is expected sometime in 1Q26, which should give some light on discussions with the FDA on a potential accelerated approval pathway. Elsewhere, WVE-007 follow-up data from INLIGHT cohorts, including those three and six-month results across multiple dose levels, should come through in mid-2026. Investors should also keep an eye on the next earnings release around March 2nd.
